title: "I'm fine" is almost never the whole story.
body: The phrase is a social shortcut. It tells the other person you don't want to be helped or asked. Sometimes that's appropriate. Sometimes it's hiding a feeling that has nowhere to go.
reflectionQuestion: When did you last say "I'm fine" and mean something else?

title: Three words for feelings is not enough.
body: Stressed / sad / angry covers maybe 10% of the real terrain. Disappointed, lonely, ashamed, jealous, contented, grieving, relieved — each is a different actionable signal. Naming precisely is half the work.
reflectionQuestion: What word would you use today if you had to be more specific than "stressed"?

title: Anger says: something here matters to you.
body: Anger is not a problem to suppress. It's a flag — usually pointing at a boundary that has been crossed or a value that has been violated. The work is to ask what it's protecting before you act on it.
reflectionQuestion: What does your current anger want to protect?

title: Sadness says: something you valued has been lost or is at risk.
body: Sadness is the body's accounting for what mattered. Trying to stop feeling sad before you've named what's lost just shifts the cost somewhere else.
reflectionQuestion: What does your current sadness know about what you've lost or are losing?

title: Fear says: prepare, don't avoid.
body: Useful fear is specific — about a real risk, with a possible plan. Unhelpful fear is global — about everything, with no exit. The work is to ask: is this fear pointing at something I can actually prepare for?
reflectionQuestion: What's your current fear pointing at — and is it something you can prepare for?

title: Guilt says "I did a bad thing." Shame says "I am a bad thing."
body: Guilt is repairable. You apologise, you change behaviour, you move on. Shame is dangerous because it bypasses the act and condemns the self. If you can convert shame into guilt, the path back opens up.
reflectionQuestion: What recent feeling — is it actually guilt (about an act) or shame (about you)?

title: Joy deserves attention too.
body: Most people savour negative emotions and rush past positive ones. The result is a skewed inner record. Spending fifteen seconds with a moment of joy — letting it land — rewrites that bias.
reflectionQuestion: What's one small good thing today that you skipped past?

title: You can feel two opposing things at once.
body: Grief and relief at a parent's death. Pride and resentment at a partner's success. Love and exhaustion for a child. Trying to pick one as the "real" feeling is what wrecks the texture of adult life.
reflectionQuestion: What two things are you currently feeling that don't fit together neatly?

title: Self-criticism doesn't produce change. Self-kindness does.
body: The clinical evidence converges: harsh inner speech predicts worse outcomes across behaviour change, recovery, performance. Speaking to yourself the way you'd speak to a friend in the same situation is the load-bearing move.
reflectionQuestion: What would you say to a friend in your current state — and what are you saying to yourself instead?

title: You will be hurt by people you love. That's not a flaw in love.
body: Closeness creates exposure. The people who can wound you deepest are the people you've let near. Pretending otherwise leads to brittle relationships. Accepting it leads to repair as a skill.
reflectionQuestion: What recent hurt from someone close — has it been named, or is it just sitting?

title: A feeling is information, not an order.
body: Feeling angry doesn't mean you should yell. Feeling afraid doesn't mean you should hide. The feeling reports what's happening; the action is your call. The pause between them is where adulthood lives.
reflectionQuestion: What feeling did you treat as an order recently — and what would the pause have changed?

title: You are the one watching the feeling, not the feeling itself.
body: Sadness arrives. Sadness leaves. You're still there. The capacity to notice a feeling without being it is the central move of Unit 3 — and the thing the rest of the course will keep cashing in on.
reflectionQuestion: What feeling have you been treating as who you are rather than what you're experiencing?
